author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
He Concludes that He Does Not Deprive the Wicked of Free Will.
title: He Concludes that He Does Not Deprive the Wicked of Free Will.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: It is not, therefore, true, as some affirm that we say, and as that correspondent of yours ventures moreover to write, that “all are forced into sin,” as if they were unwilling, “by the necessity of their flesh;” but if they are already of the age to use the choice of their own mind, they are both retained in sin by their own will, and by their own will are hurried along from sin to sin.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For even he who persuades and deceives does not act in them, except that they may commit sin by their will, either by ignorance of the truth or by delight in iniquity, or by both evils,—as well of blindness as of weakness.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: But this will, which is free in evil things because it takes pleasure in evil, is not free in good things, for the reason that it has not been made free.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: Nor can a man will any good thing unless he is aided by Him who cannot will evil,—that is, by the grace of God through Jesus Christ our Lord.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: For “everything which is not of faith is sin.”

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And thus the good will which withdraws itself from sin is faithful, because the just lives by faith.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And it pertains to faith to believe on Christ.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: And no man can believe on Christ—that is, come to Him—unless it be given to him.

verses.book: A Treatise Against Two Letters of the Pelagians.
verses.text: No man, therefore, can have a righteous will, unless, with no foregoing merits, he has received the true, that is, the gratuitous grace from above.
